name: Aslam ibn Amr
title: The Turkish Servant
shortDescription: The Turkish-speaking servant and scribe of Imam Hussain (AS) whose cheek the Imam lovingly placed his face upon in his final moments.
fullStory:
Aslam ibn Amr (in some sources Wadih al-Turki or Aslam al-Turki) was a Turkish-speaking servant whom Imam Hussain (AS) purchased after the passing of his brother Imam Hasan (AS) and gifted to his son Imam Sajjad (AS). Besides serving, Aslam was also the Imam's scribe and was fluent in both Arabic and Turkish. He accompanied the Hussaini caravan from Medina to Karbala and was considered one of the loyal and close companions of the Ahl al-Bayt.

On the day of Ashura, when it was the companions' turn to sacrifice themselves, Aslam asked Imam Hussain (AS) for permission to enter the battlefield. Despite being a servant, he entered the field with unparalleled bravery and recited heroic war poetry: 'The sea boils from the strike of my sword, and the sky becomes filled with blood from my flying arrows'.

Aslam fought with great skill and brought down many enemies until he was severely wounded by multiple blows and fell to the ground. While there was still a breath of life in him, Imam Hussain (AS) rushed to his side.

The Imam sat by this Turkish servant, embraced his head, and, just as he did with his son Ali Akbar, placed his holy face upon Aslam's bloody cheek. Aslam, who was dying, opened his eyes, smiled, and proudly said: 'Who is like me, that the son of the Messenger of God has placed his cheek upon mine?'

After uttering these words, Aslam's pure soul flew to the heavens. The Imam's loving behavior towards this Turkish servant, identical to his behavior towards his son Ali Akbar, depicted one of the most beautiful manifestations of human equality and the rejection of racism in the school of Ashura.
quoteArabic: مَنْ مِثْلي وَابْنُ رَسُولِ اللَّهِ واضِعٌ خَدَّهُ عَلى خَدّي؟
quote: Who is like me, that the son of the Messenger of God has placed his cheek upon mine?
